title: Flaxseed Breakfast Pudding

ingredients.name: flax seeds, ground (or whole)
visual_checks: ground absorbs liquid better; grind in mixie if needed
ingredients.notes: ground absorbs liquid better; grind in mixie if needed

subs.name: No direct substitute — flax seeds provide the gelling; without them this recipe does not set
cold water, cold oat milk or any plant milk
subs.notes: use full-fat coconut milk for a richer pudding

subs.name: honey (no longer vegan) or sugar
subs.notes: 2 tsp sugar per serving is plenty

steps.text: If using whole flax seeds, grind them coarsely in a mixie or coffee grinder — ground flax absorbs liquid faster and creates a smoother pudding. Whole seeds can be used but take longer to swell and will have a crunchier texture.
Whisk the ground flax seeds, coconut milk, jaggery, cardamom and vanilla together in a bowl until the jaggery dissolves. The mixture will look thin at this stage — that is correct.

Divide the mixture into two jars or containers, cover and refrigerate for at least 4 hours, or overnight. The flax seeds absorb the liquid and swell into a thick, spoonable pudding with a texture similar to tapioca or chia pudding.
Remove from the fridge and stir well. If the pudding is too thick, loosen with a splash of water or coconut milk. Top with sliced banana and chopped almonds. Serve cold.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: coconut-milk → cold water, cold oat milk or any plant milk (use full-fat coconut milk for a richer pudding); jaggery → honey (no longer vegan) or sugar (2 tsp sugar per serving is plenty); flax-seeds → No direct substitute — flax seeds provide the gelling; without them this recipe does not set
indian_kitchen_adaptation: Flax seeds (alsi/ali vidhai) are sold at every kirana store and health-food shop in India — they are far cheaper here than in Western markets. Grind a batch in a mixie dry-jar and keep in the fridge. Coconut milk (thengai paal) from a carton or freshly squeezed from grated coconut both work. Cardamom (elaichi/elakkai) and jaggery make this taste like a South Indian dessert for breakfast. Refrigerate in a steel container overnight.
